title: Delhi gets respite from heat
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds") Delhi
full_text:
New Delhi: The city got a much needed respite from extreme heat Wednesday.
The maximum temperature at Safdarjung was 34.8 degrees Celsius, 2.6 notches below normal and significantly lower than 40.5 degrees Celsius a day earlier.
The day saw less humid weather. The feels-like temperature was 43.6 degrees Celsius at 5.30 pm against 53.5 degrees a day earlier.
The Met has issued a yellow alert for rain and thunderstorm for Wednesday and Thursday. However, no rain happened till late Wednesday afternoon.
“
“Conditions are favourable for a further advance of the southwest monsoon into parts of Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh Haryana-Chandigarh-Delhi and Punjab, and some parts of Rajasthan during next 2 days,” IMD said.
The maximum is likely to be 32-34 degrees Celsius Thursday and Friday.

title: Delhi weather is testing everyone’s patience: Tips to beat humidity when it feels like 53°C - News9live
full_text:
Delhi weather is testing everyone’s patience: Tips to beat humidity when it feels like 53°C
Delhi's 'feels like' temperature has touched nearly 53°C due to extreme humidity. Discover 10 doctor-backed tips to beat Delhi's humid weather, prevent heat exhaustion, stay hydrated and protect yourself during dangerous heat index conditions.
New Delhi: Delhi’s weather has taken an uncomfortable turn, with many residents feeling as though they are living inside a steam room. While the actual temperature may hover around 35–40°C, the ‘feels like’ temperature, also known as the heat index, has recently climbed beyond 51°C and even touched nearly 53°C because of extremely high humidity. This happens when moisture in the air prevents sweat from evaporating efficiently, making it much harder for the body to cool itself naturally. Meteorologists explain that humid air, high dew points and rising wet-bulb temperatures combine to create dangerous conditions, increasing the risk of dehydration, heat exhaustion and heatstroke. With monsoon moisture arriving before widespread rainfall, Delhiites are experiencing sticky, oppressive weather that feels significantly hotter than the thermometer suggests.
